stytch.name: Stytch
stytch.short_description: Stytch is an identity platform providing authentication, authorization, and fraud prevention APIs for humans and AI agents.
stytch.starting_price: $0/month (Pay As You Go, free up to 10,000 MAUs)

features.description: SAML and OIDC single sign-on plus SCIM provisioning, with the first 5 SSO/SCIM connections included free.
features.title: Enterprise SSO & SCIM

features.description: Passkeys, magic links, and one-time passcodes delivered via SMS and email, with automatic provider failover for delivery reliability.
features.title: Passwordless authentication

features.description: Authentication for service-to-service calls and AI agents, with 1,000 M2M tokens included in the free tier.
features.title: Machine-to-machine (M2M) authentication

features.description: Device fingerprinting and intelligent rate limiting, described as achieving 99.99% bot detection accuracy, with 10,000 free fingerprints per month.
features.title: Fraud and bot protection

features.description: Organization-level policies, RBAC, and SCIM support for building B2B applications with unlimited organizations, even on the free tier.
features.title: Turnkey multi-tenancy

features.description: A self-service portal that lets enterprise customers manage their own SSO and SCIM connections.
features.title: Embeddable Admin Portal

features.description: Support for cross-application integrations between a company's own products and third-party apps.
features.title: Connected Apps

features.description: Prebuilt, customizable login UI components alongside headless SDKs and backend APIs for full flexibility.
features.title: Web & mobile UI components

pros: Free tier includes 10,000 monthly active users/AI agents, unlimited organizations, 5 SSO/SCIM connections, and 1,000 M2M tokens
Pricing has no hard caps or pricing cliffs, so overages are billed for actual usage only
Explicit support for machine-to-machine and AI agent authentication, not just human end users
Broad auth method coverage including passkeys, OAuth, SSO, sessions, and RBAC in the base tier
Backed by Twilio after Stytch joined the company, adding messaging infrastructure depth
cons: SSO/SCIM connections beyond the free 5 cost $125 per connection, similar to competitors
Enterprise pricing, including HIPAA/BAA compliance and a 99.99% uptime SLA, is custom and not publicly listed
Brand customization requires a separate $99 one-time fee
Fraud prevention fingerprinting beyond the free 10,000 is billed per use at $0.005 each

faqs.answer: Yes. The Pay As You Go plan is free up to 10,000 monthly active users/AI agents, 5 SSO or SCIM connections, and 1,000 M2M tokens.
faqs.question: Is Stytch free to use?

faqs.answer: Yes, Stytch positions itself as an identity platform for both humans and AI agents, and its free tier explicitly covers AI agents alongside human monthly active users.
faqs.question: Does Stytch support authentication for AI agents?

faqs.answer: Stytch charges usage-based overages with no hard caps or pricing cliffs, plus $125 per additional SSO/SCIM connection beyond the 5 included free.
faqs.question: What happens if usage exceeds the free tier?

faqs.answer: Yes, Stytch has joined Twilio to build what the company describes as the intelligent identity layer for the internet.
faqs.question: Is Stytch part of Twilio?

faqs.answer: Yes, via device fingerprinting (10,000 free per month, then $0.005 each) and intelligent rate limiting for bot detection.
faqs.question: Does Stytch offer fraud protection?

trufflehog.name: TruffleHog
trufflehog.short_description: TruffleHog is a secret-scanning tool from Truffle Security Co. that finds and verifies exposed credentials across git repos, Docker images, and cloud storage.

features.description: Scans git version history, Docker images, and comments to find exposed secrets, not just the latest commit.
features.title: Deep repository scanning

features.description: Programmatically verifies discovered secrets against the relevant service/API to eliminate false positives.
features.title: Live credential verification

features.description: Ships with detectors for 800+ secret types out of the box.
features.title: 800+ secret detectors

features.description: Analyzes all branches in a repository, not just the primary branch.
features.title: Multi-branch scanning

features.description: Blocks secrets from being committed or pushed in the first place.
features.title: Pre-commit and pre-receive hooks

features.description: Automatically identifies the resources and permissions tied to a found API key or secret.
features.title: TruffleHog Analyze

features.description: Supports custom regex rules in addition to built-in detectors.
features.title: Custom regex support

features.description: Tracks the status of found keys and sends alert reminders with rotation guidance.
features.title: Remediation tracking

pros: Free, open-source core with 800+ built-in secret detectors
Verifies secrets live against real services/APIs to cut down false positives
Scans across full git history, multiple branches, and Docker images, not just the latest commit
Pre-commit and pre-receive hook support to prevent leaks before they happen
Enterprise tier adds SSO, RBAC, a management dashboard, and 20+ integrations for larger teams
cons: Enterprise pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting sales
Dashboard, continuous monitoring, SSO/RBAC, and most integrations are Enterprise-only, not in the open-source version
Add-ons like TruffleHog Analyze and Forager are sold separately from the core scanner

faqs.answer: The open-source core is free, offering GitHub, S3, directory, GCS, and Docker scanning with 800+ detectors.
faqs.question: Is TruffleHog free to use?

faqs.answer: It scans GitHub, S3, local directories, GCS, and Docker images/history for exposed secrets.
faqs.question: What does TruffleHog scan?

faqs.answer: Yes, it uses programmatic verification against the relevant service or API to confirm a secret is live, reducing false positives.
faqs.question: Does TruffleHog verify the secrets it finds?

faqs.answer: Enterprise adds 20+ integrations (GitHub, Confluence, JIRA, Slack, etc.), on-premises/cloud deployment choice, continuous monitoring, a management dashboard, SSO, RBAC, and priority support.
faqs.question: What does the Enterprise tier add?

faqs.answer: TruffleHog is made by Truffle Security Co., founded in 2021.
faqs.question: Who makes TruffleHog?

features: GitHub, S3, directory, GCS, and Docker scanning
GitHub Actions, pre-commit, and pre-receive hooks

1password-secrets-automation.name: 1Password Secrets Automation
1password-secrets-automation.short_description: A 1Password developer feature for automating secrets access in apps and cloud infrastructure via Service Accounts or self-hosted Connect servers.

features.description: An authentication method for the 1Password CLI that isn't tied to an individual user, letting teams automate secrets management without deploying additional infrastructure.
features.title: Service Accounts

features.description: A self-hosted alternative that caches data in your own infrastructure, giving more control, scalability, and reduced dependency on the 1Password API, plus REST API access not available with Service Accounts.
features.title: Connect servers

features.description: Both Service Accounts and Connect servers work through the 1Password CLI for scripting and automation.
features.title: 1Password CLI integration

features.description: Pre-built integrations sync secrets into pipelines such as GitHub Actions, CircleCI, and Jenkins, with the ability to build custom integrations.
features.title: CI/CD pipeline integrations

features.description: Documented support for syncing secrets into Kubernetes environments.
features.title: Kubernetes integration

features.description: Developers can reference secrets stored in 1Password instead of hardcoding credentials in application code.
features.title: Secret references

features.description: Extensions help developers work with APIs and databases while detecting exposed secrets before they're committed to a repository.
features.title: IDE extensions

pros: Included with a 1Password subscription rather than sold as a separate line item
Service Accounts require no additional infrastructure to deploy
Connect servers give teams control over caching and reduce reliance on 1Password's API availability
Pre-built CI/CD integrations for common tools like GitHub Actions, CircleCI, and Jenkins
CLI-based workflow supports automating tasks such as credential rotation
cons: Service Accounts use a rate-limited access model
Connect servers must be self-hosted by the customer, adding operational overhead
REST API access is only available through Connect, not Service Accounts
No dedicated public pricing page separate from general 1Password subscription plans

faqs.answer: It is a set of tools for securely accessing 1Password items and vaults from a company's applications and cloud infrastructure, using either Service Accounts or Connect servers.
faqs.question: What is 1Password Secrets Automation?

faqs.answer: Service Accounts are a low-overhead, rate-limited authentication method for the 1Password CLI with no infrastructure to deploy, while Connect servers are self-hosted, cache data locally, and expose a REST API.
faqs.question: What is the difference between Service Accounts and Connect servers?

faqs.answer: According to 1Password's documentation, it is included with a 1Password subscription rather than billed separately.
faqs.question: Does Secrets Automation cost extra?

faqs.answer: 1Password documents pre-built integrations for GitHub Actions, CircleCI, and Jenkins, along with support for building custom integrations.
faqs.question: Which CI/CD tools integrate with Secrets Automation?

features.description: Provides visibility across human-to-agent-to-tool-to-data authorization chains.

features.description: Uses the open-source Open Policy Agent and OPAL projects as its policy engine foundation, available managed or self-hosted.

openfga.name: OpenFGA
openfga.short_description: Open-source, CNCF-incubating engine for fine-grained, relationship-based authorization inspired by Google Zanzibar.

features.description: Uses an authorization modeling language, based on concepts from Google's Zanzibar paper, to define relationship-based access control between users and resources.
features.title: Zanzibar-inspired ReBAC modeling

features.description: Designed to answer authorization check calls in milliseconds, suitable for use in the request path of applications and APIs.
features.title: Millisecond authorization checks

features.description: Supports role-based, attribute-based, and relationship-based access control patterns within the same system.
features.title: Multi-model support (RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC)

features.description: Official SDKs are available for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java, alongside a CLI and direct HTTP API access.
features.title: Multi-language SDKs

features.description: Runs as a standalone OpenFGA server that teams can deploy themselves, for example by pulling the official Docker image.
features.title: Self-hosted server via Docker

features.description: Provides Check, Read, Expand, and ListObjects operations for querying and evaluating relationship tuples.
features.title: Relationship query API

features.description: Developed with a transparent RFC process and public roadmap, and maintained by contributors including engineers from Okta, Grafana Labs, and Gitpod.
features.title: Community governance

pros: Free and fully open source, with no licensing cost
CNCF incubating project, providing vendor-neutral governance
Adopted in production by organizations such as Auth0, Okta, Docker, and Grafana
SDKs for several popular languages reduce integration effort
Flexible enough to model RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC in one system
cons: Requires self-hosting and operating your own OpenFGA server; openfga.dev does not document a managed hosted offering
Zanzibar-style relationship modeling has a learning curve for teams unfamiliar with the approach
As a community open-source project, formal commercial support is not part of the core openfga.dev offering

faqs.answer: OpenFGA is an open-source authorization system that lets developers implement fine-grained, relationship-based access control using an authorization modeling language and API.
faqs.question: What is OpenFGA?

faqs.answer: Yes. OpenFGA is free and open source, and can be self-hosted (for example via Docker) with no licensing fees.
faqs.question: Is OpenFGA free to use?

faqs.answer: OpenFGA is a CNCF incubating project. It was originally created by Auth0/Okta and open sourced in 2022, and is now maintained by a broader community including engineers from Grafana Labs and Gitpod.
faqs.question: Who maintains OpenFGA?

faqs.answer: OpenFGA supports relationship-based access control (ReBAC) inspired by Google's Zanzibar paper, as well as role-based (RBAC) and attribute-based (ABAC) access control patterns.
faqs.question: What authorization models does OpenFGA support?

faqs.answer: OpenFGA offers SDKs for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java, in addition to a CLI and a direct HTTP API.
faqs.question: What SDKs does OpenFGA provide?

features: Full relationship-query API access (Check, Read, Expand, ListObjects)
SDKs for Node.js, Go, .NET, Python, and Java
